title: Week 1: Remote Sensing Basics
text:
Summary
This week lecture introduces the basic concepts of remote sensing and we set up workflows for work with Sentinel-2 and Landsat imagery in QGIS, SNAP and R software.
I learn about different satellite sensors such as Sentinel-2 MSI and Landsat 8/9 OLI, which have varying spatial, spectral, temporal and radiometric resolutions, and why these trade-offs is important when choose data for study cities or environmental changes. For example, Sentinel-2 have 10m spatial resolution for some bands but Landsat is 30m, and this affects what detail we can see in urban areas.
In practical session, we download Sentinel-2 Level-2A surface reflectance data for a select city, locate the 10m bands, and create our own true-colour composite by merging bottom-of-atmosphere (BOA) bands into multiband GeoTIFF file using QGIS, instead of use the pre-pack TCI image with 0-255 values. This shows me how to control display and processing step by step.
Then we move to SNAP, which is sensor-aware GIS tool, load Sentinel product direct from zip file, experiment with band combinations like true colour, false colour, atmospheric penetration and SWIR composites to see different features. We also do resampling from 10m to 20m, masking with vector polygons for specific areas, and generate simple image statistics plus scatterplots for understand spectral feature space. After that, export masked subsets of Sentinel and Landsat data plus shapefiles for land-cover classes like bare earth, grass, forest, low-urban and high-urban to R.
Using terra package, we extract pixel values by class with terra::extract, tidy data with tidyverse, calculate band-wise means and standard deviations per sensor and class. Plot spectral profiles with error bars, density plots, and even t-test on urban reflectance between sensors make abstract idea of electromagnetic radiation, scattering and spectral signatures more concrete.
I have question: is 10m resolution enough for small urban green spaces or informal settlements? Benefit of this is free global data archive with good temporal coverage, but limitation include cloud cover in optical imagery and need for careful resampling to avoid mix pixel values. Overall, practical link theory to hands-on analysis well.
Applications
Remote sensing data from Landsat and Sentinel-2 is widely used in urban and environmental research plus policy making, because provide multi-temporal free archives for track changes over large areas. Landsat with 30m resolution and 16-day revisit, Sentinel-2 at 10-20m and 5-day revisit support study like forest loss, illegal logging, urban green-space mapping, flood response and urban heat island effect.
Researcher create spectral indices such NDVI for vegetation health, NDBI for built-up areas, and others from spectral index database, which feed into global product like Dynamic World for near-real-time 10m land-cover classification. This approach vital for policy, allow cities and government monitor progress on Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), climate mitigation plan and disaster risk framework without rely on slow expensive ground surveys or administrative data.
For instance, consistent calibrated measure of vegetation greenness and surface moisture inform drought early-warning system or evaluate if afforestation, wetland restoration project actually visible in landscape over time. In urban context, these method help assess green infrastructure effectiveness or track impervious surface growth relate to flood risk.
More advance technique from practical like Tasseled Cap transformation and principal component analysis (PCA)-style reduction connect direct to peer-review literature on urban classification and change detection. Tasseled Cap component—brightness, greenness, wetness—original for Landsat but adaptable to Sentinel-2 when bands match spectrally, give compact indices that separate urban fabric, vegetation and water or bare soil more cleanly than raw bands. This widely apply to map urban expansion, identify high-biomass vs low-biomass area, detect moisture change for metropolitan climate strategy or risk mapping.
Lecture mention SAR and polarization briefly, which literature use for see through clouds, monitor flood, landslide or urban infrastructure deformation—crucial in tropical or cloudy region where optical data have temporal gap. The full workflow we follow—atmospheric correction to surface reflectance, careful resampling, masking to policy-relevant boundary like administrative unit, then extract and statistically compare spectral signature—mirror structure in many publish EO case study and policy report on urban environmental challenge. Different study take vary approach: some focus supervised classification with machine learning on transform bands, other use time-series analysis for change detect, but all emphasize pre-processing quality for reliable result.
Reflection
This week teaching make me realise that remote sensing not just about download pretty satellite image, but control long chain of decision from atmospheric scattering and surface interaction, through resampling, masking to how summarise and visualise spectral signatures in analysis.
The four resolution types—spatial, temporal, spectral, radiometric—directly limit what urban question we can realistically answer and at what scale, for example fine 10m detail good for park mapping but maybe not individual building in dense city.
It realistic to integrate Earth Observation (EO) into policy like urban planning or environmental governance, especially with cloud platform like Google Earth Engine that handle big data process, but constraint such data download time, require specialist skill, cloud contamination and computational resource could be problem for small government or researcher in develop country.
Personal, I find spectral profile comparison between Sentinel and Landsat most interesting, because show how same land cover like high-urban look different across sensor due to band pass and radiometric resolution—this why important match sensor for time-series study. Also benefit to see error bar from standard deviation, give sense of variability within class that raw image not show.
For my future as urban spatial science student at UCL, this tool and workflow very useful, because I can apply to research on Airbnb impact, short-term rental regulation or commercial real estate analysis by extract spectral signature for urban fabric like dense informal settlement vs high-rise CBD, then link to policy target such SDG 11 sustainable city.
Even if I not use SNAP every time due to prefer Python or R fully, the concept grounding—how sensor work, what spectral signature mean, why resampling and masking matter—will stay with me for design EO-base analysis in master thesis or job at consult like CBRE. Maybe similar tool like ESA SNAP evolve to more user-friend for non-expert policymaker.
Overall, this week spark interest in combine EO with my GIS and data science skill for real-world urban problem.

title: Introduction
text:
Introduction
This is a book created from markdown and executable code.
See @knuth84 for additional discussion of literate programming.

1 + 1

[1] 2

title: Week 2: Portfolio Tools
text:
Summary
This week lecture focus on portfolio tools for presentation and documentation, specifically Xaringan for slide and Quarto for individual portfolio like book or website. We learn how use Xaringan, which create by Yihui Xie, install from GitHub with remotes::install_github(“yihui/xaringan”), then make new project and use template from R Markdown “Ninja Presentation”. Key feature include yaml header for title, output as xaringan::moon_reader with lib_dir: libs and nature option for highlight code. Theme change by css like default, metropolis or use xaringanthemer package for custom color. Slide control with — for new slide, – for incremental reveal, class: inverse for flip color. List use - or number, pull-left and pull-right for side by side, image with knitr::include_graphics or fig chunk. Equation like NDVI= (NIR-Red)/(NIR+Red) use mathmath. Addin “Infinite Moon Reader” for live preview when save file.
Practical task is create 5-9 slide Xaringan on any sensor and host on GitHub for mark like learning diary. For Quarto, download from quarto.org, new project “Quarto Book” generate index.qmd and _quarto.yml for config title, chapter list, bibliography and format html/pdf. Syntax similar RMarkdown but support Python/R/Julia, visual editor option, figure side by side with ::: {#fig layout-ncol=2}. I skip SNAP because cannot open file from it, so only work on QGIS for previous week image process. Attached screenshot show QGIS with Sentinel-2 band like B04 B03 B02 stack for true colour, layer panel with granule, also RGB composite tool open and histogram. Other picture similar but different colour band combination like false colour. Benefit is easy share code online but limitation need Git skill and theme sometime hard custom. Question: how Quarto compare Bookdown for thesis? ## Xaringan Presentation




Applications
Xaringan and Quarto widely apply in academia, data science job and teaching for reproducible presentation and portfolio. Xaringan use for conference talk, workshop or class like UCL CASA module because support live code run, incremental slide and theme easy change with CSS or xaringanthemer. Garrick Aden-Buie xaringanExtra add search, clipboard, tile view or code hover, make interactive for audience. Example in literature or blog, Alison Hill “Making slides in R Markdown” show how build xaringan for teach R, Garrick site pkg.garrickadenbuie.com/xaringanExtra demo extension. Policy or industry report use similar for share analysis with embed code and figure auto update. Quarto replace Bookdown for multi-language book, website or slide, like workshop book now Quarto not Bookdown. Advantage over Bookdown is less complex yaml, one _quarto.yml for config, output anything html/pdf/blog without extra package.
In study, researcher use Quarto for reproducible paper with quarto.org doc, or portfolio show GitHub project like RStudio quarto tool page. Table make easy with kableExtra from excel data, panelset for code/table/output tab. GitHub Pages host free after commit to /docs or /root branch, essential for CV or job apply data scientist. Vary approach in resource: some stick Xaringan for R only slide, other Quarto for full site with chapter. Sydney Xaringan theme or metropolis for professional look, video tutorial like YouTube “xaringan” explain CSS custom. For urban spatial like my field, can use for present GIS analysis, embed map or sensor data plot from QGIS export since SNAP skip. Overall workflow render, git push, Pages deploy mirror real collab in team like Deloitte project, where share reproducible report important for stakeholder.


Reflection
This week skill in Xaringan and Quarto place in broader context of reproducible research and portfolio build, essential for graduate job in urban data science or consult like CBRE where employer want see code not just word. Realistic integrate these tool to policy or academic workflow because free, GitHub host and live preview speed develop, but constraint like learn yaml/Git first time or RStudio update need for Quarto, also visual editor sometime glitch with complex code. For me interesting is xaringanExtra search function and Quarto multi-language, because future may use Python for ML on remote sensing data link to urban planning. Skip SNAP no big issue since QGIS handle stack band fine, screenshot show layer with B04 red, B03 green, B02 blue for RGB composite, useful for true colour map city like attach image Hong Kong area with land sea urban.
Personal reflection, as UCL master student in urban spatial science, this tool very useful future for thesis present or group project GitHub, embed Xaringan in Quarto portfolio show learning diary and sensor presentation. Even not use every week, concept like incremental slide or panelset for code will help communicate analysis to non-technical like policymaker on Airbnb regulation or real estate. Compare previous remote sensing, portfolio tool complement by let document practical output like QGIS screenshot with code explanation. Might not use Bookdown now Quarto better, but similar skill transfer. Overall excite have online portfolio by end week, good for IELTS improve English academic write and fitness travel balance with quick render.

title: Understanding Our Eyes in the Sky
text:
1.1 The Two Ways Sensors “See”
Think of sensors as the eyes of a satellite. Just like our own eyes need light to see, satellite sensors need energy. However, they get that energy in two very different ways.

Passive Sensors: The “Daylight Photographers”
Passive sensors are like your smartphone camera on a sunny day. They don’t provide their own light; instead, they wait for the Sun to shine on the Earth and then “catch” the energy that bounces back (reflection) or the heat the Earth gives off (emission).

The Pro: They are great for seeing vegetation health and surface temperatures.
The Con: They are “light-dependent.” If it’s nighttime or if there’s a thick layer of clouds, a passive sensor is essentially blind.







NoteAnalogy



Imagine taking a photo of a mountain at noon. You are using a passive system (the sun). If the sun goes down, your photo is black.




Active Sensors: The “Flashlight Users”
Active sensors bring their own energy source to the party. They beam a signal (like a laser or a radio wave) down to the ground and measure how long it takes to bounce back.

The Pro: Because they provide their own “light,” they can see through thick clouds, smoke, and even total darkness. This is why Radar (SAR) is so popular for monitoring tropical regions where it’s always cloudy.
The Con: They require much more power to operate.

title: Understanding Our Eyes in the Sky
text:
1.2 The Four Dimensions of Resolution
Not all satellite data is created equal. To know if a dataset is “good enough” for your project, you have to look at these four resolutions. Think of these as the “specs” of your data.

1.2.1 Spatial Resolution: “How sharp is the image?”
This refers to the size of the smallest “pixel” on the ground. - High Resolution: You can see individual cars or trees (e.g., 30cm to 1m). - Low Resolution: One pixel might cover an entire neighborhood (e.g., 1km). It’s great for global weather but terrible for urban planning.


1.2.2 Spectral Resolution: “What colors can it see?”
Human eyes see in Red, Green, and Blue. But sensors can “see” invisible colors like Infrared or Ultraviolet. - A sensor with high spectral resolution can tell the difference between a plastic roof and a metal roof because it picks up subtle “signatures” in the light that we can’t see.


1.2.3 Temporal Resolution: “How often does it look?”
This is the “revisit time.” If a satellite passes over your house today, how many days until it comes back? - For tracking a fast-moving forest fire, you need high temporal resolution (hourly). - For tracking urban growth over a decade, once a month is plenty.


1.2.4 Radiometric Resolution: “How many shades of gray?”
This is about the sensitivity of the sensor. Imagine a black-and-white photo. - Low Radiometric Resolution: You only see black and white. - High Radiometric Resolution: You can see 1,000 different shades of gray. This allows us to see tiny differences in water depth or soil moisture that would otherwise look the same.

title: Understanding Our Eyes in the Sky
text:
1.3 Personal Reflection
Starting this journey, I used to think of satellite imagery as just a static map. But realizing that we can “tune” these four resolutions—spatial, spectral, temporal, and radiometric—makes me see Earth Observation more like a diagnostic tool.
Choosing the right sensor is a balancing act. You usually can’t have “perfect” resolution in all four categories at once; you have to pick the one that fits your story. Whether I’m looking at the urban heat in Lisboa or the greenery of a local park, the sensor I choose dictates the truth I find.
